content:
Bayes' theorem addresses a problem that predates its name: **how to reason from effects back to causes** when you only know forward probabilities.

**Thomas Bayes** (c. 1701–1761), an English Presbyterian minister and mathematician, worked on inverse probability—inferring the chance of a cause from observed outcomes. His manuscript *An Essay towards solving a Problem in the Doctrine of Chances* was completed around 1763 but published posthumously, edited by **Richard Price**.

**Pierre-Simon Laplace** independently rediscovered and greatly extended the same reasoning in the late 18th century, applying it to astronomy, demography, and error analysis. Laplace's version popularized the formula across continental Europe and tied it to scientific inference.

For most of the 19th and early 20th centuries, inverse probability competed with **frequentist** methods championed by **Fisher**, **Neyman**, and **Pearson**. Fisher's **maximum likelihood estimation** offered an alternative route to parameter inference without explicit priors.

The mid-20th-century **Bayesian revival**—driven by computing and practitioners like **Savage**, **Jeffreys**, and later **Box**, **Turing** (wartime code-breaking), and **Good**—restored Bayes' theorem as a practical tool once machines could evaluate integrals and sums at scale.

Today the same identity underpins spam filters, medical diagnostics, Naive Bayes classifiers, Bayesian optimization, and approximate inference in large generative models.

timeline.label: Bayes & Price (1763)
timeline.description: Posthumous publication of Bayes' essay on inverse probability; Price communicates the result to the Royal Society.

timeline.label: Laplace (1774–1812)
timeline.description: Independent formulation and broad application of what is now called Bayes' theorem in *Théorie analytique des probabilités*.

timeline.label: Frequentist era (1900s)
timeline.description: Fisher's likelihood framework and Neyman-Pearson testing offer competing inference paradigms; inverse probability falls out of mainstream statistics temporarily.

timeline.label: Bayesian revival & computing (1950s–present)
timeline.description: Growing compute power enables Bayesian updating at scale; theorem becomes central to ML classifiers, A/B testing, and modern probabilistic AI.
